---
title: "Working with <br>Strings"
subtitle: Day 12
title-slide-attributes:
  data-background-image: ../img/horst_stringr.jpeg
  data-background-size: 70%
  data-background-position: bottom right
  data-background-opacity: "0.8"
  data-slide-number: none
format: 
  revealjs:
    incremental: true
    code-copy: true
    code-link: true
auto-stretch: false
editor_options: 
  chunk_output_type: console
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, message = FALSE, warning = FALSE)

library(countdown)
library(tidyverse)
library(lubridate)
library(palmerpenguins)
library(patchwork)
library(ggthemes)
library(nycflights23)
library(here)
slides_theme = theme_minimal(
  base_family = "Atkinson Hyperlegible",
  base_size = 16)

theme_set(slides_theme)
```

## Today

1. Lab Quiz 2 Info
2. Local versions of R/RStudio/GitHub
3. Intro to strings

# string {.maize}

any finite sequence of characters (i.e., letters, numerals, symbols and punctuation marks).

## Strings in R 

Anything surrounded by quotes(`"`) or single quotes(`'`).

```{r}
#| eval: false
"Carleton College"
"2025"
'"Hello World"'
```

## `str_view`

`str_view` is a handy function that prints the underlying representation of a string and can also be used to check pattern matching (which we'll see in a bit!) 

```{r}
str_view("Carleton College")
str_view("2025")
str_view('"Hello World"')
```


## 

The "escape" backslash \ is used to escape the special use of certain characters

```{r}
#| eval: false
str_view("Math\Stats")
```
```
Error: '\S' is an unrecognized escape in character string (<input>:1:16)
```

. . . 

```{r}
str_view("\"")
str_view("\\")
str_view("Math\\Stats")
```

## {.center}

::::: columns
::: {.column .nonincremental width="50%"}

![](https://stringr.tidyverse.org/logo.png)
:::

:::{.column .nonincremental width="50%"}
- Simple, consistent functions for working with strings.

- Part of the `tidyverse`

```{r}
# loaded with tidyverse
library(stringr)
```

:::
:::::

## String length

`str_length()` determines the length of a string.

```{r}
cc <- "Carleton College"
str_length(cc)
```

## Combine strings

`str_c()` allows us to easily create strings from variables/vectors.


```{r}
#| output-location: fragment


building <- "CMC"
room <- "306"
begin_time <- "11:10 a.m."
end_time <- "12:20 p.m."
days <- "MW"
class <- "STAT 220"
str_c(class, "meets from", begin_time, "to", end_time, 
      days, "in", building, room, sep=" ")
```

## Concatenate Strings

`str_c()` works with vectors

```{r}
letters
```

. . . 

```{r}
str_c(letters, 1:26)
```

. . . 

```{r}
str_c(letters, 1:26, sep = "")
```

. . . 


```{r}
str_c(letters, 1:26, sep = "-")
```

## Case conversion

`str_to_lower()` and `str_to_upper()` can help "fix" the case

```{r}
text <- "NoRthFieLd, mN"
str_to_lower(text)
```

. . . 

```{r}
str_to_upper(text)
```

## Babynames

```{r}
library(babynames)
babynames
```

## Popularity of "Amanda" over time

```{r}
#| echo: false
babynames %>%
  filter(name == "Amanda") %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = year, y = prop, col = sex)) +
  geom_line(size = 1.5) + 
  scale_color_colorblind() 
```

::: {.task}
Replicate this plot with your own name. If your name doesn't have enough data, try a friend or professor's name
:::

## Babynames
::::: columns
::: {.column .nonincremental width="50%"}
```{r}
babynames
```
:::

:::{.column .nonincremental .fragment width="35%" style="text-align: right;"}
Example questions: 

- How many names end in a vowel? 
- How many names contain the pattern "stat"
- How many names contain 3 A's?

:::

:::::


## Extract substrings

```{r eval=FALSE}
str_sub(string, start = 1, end = -1)
```

- `string` = character vector
- `start` / `end` = position of the first and last characters

## Extract substrings

We can pull apart strings from the start...


```{r}
cc <- "Carleton College"
str_sub(cc, 10)  # end defaults to last character
```

. . . 

```{r}
str_sub(cc, 1, 8)
```
 
. . . 

```{r}
# match the elements of each vector for positions
str_sub(cc, c(1, 10), c(8, 16))
```


## Extract substrings

... or the end

```{r}
cc <- "Carleton College"
str_sub(cc, -3)
```

. . . 

```{r}
str_sub(cc, -8, -3)
```

## Your turn: 

::: task
What will the following commands return?

:::

```{r eval=FALSE}
str_sub("Amanda Luby", 1, 4)
str_sub("Amanda Luby", 4)
str_sub("Amanda Luby", -5)
```


```{r echo=FALSE}
countdown(2)
```

## Your turn (again):

::: {.task .nonincremental}
Confer with folks around you. Fill in the blanks of the .Rmd file to...

1. Isolate the last letter of every name

2. Create a logical variable that displays whether the last letter is one of "a", "e", "i", "o", "u", or "y".

3. Use a weighted mean to calculate the proportion of children whose name ends in a vowel by year (see `?weighted.mean`)

4. and then display the results as a line plot.
:::

```{r echo=FALSE}
countdown(6)
```

## Extract substrings

What about a vector of strings?


```{r}
fruits <- c("apple", "pineapple", "Pear", "orange", "peach", "banana")
str_sub(fruits, 2, 4)
```

. . . 


```{r}
str_sub(fruits, 2, 2)
```

. . . 


```{r}
str_sub(fruits, 4, 4)
```

. . . 


```{r}
str_sub(fruits, c(2, 4, 2, 4, 2, 4), c(2, 4, 2, 4, 2, 4))
```


## Pad strings

We can add character(s) to the beginning or end of a string

```{r}
nums <- 1:10
as.character(nums)
```

. . . 

```{r}
str_pad(nums, 2, pad ="0")
```

. . . 

```{r}
str_pad(nums, 3, pad ="0")
```

. . . 

```{r}
str_pad(nums, 3, pad ="0", side = "right")
```

. . . 

```{r}
str_pad(nums, 3, pad ="0", side = "both")
```

## {.smaller}

::: {.task .nonincremental}

Use the `courses` dataset in the .rmd file to answer the following:

(a) How many of the course numbers end in `.00`? Use `str_detect()` or `str_count()` to help you answer this question.


(b) The section number appears after the decimal point. Use `mutate()` and `str_sub()` to create a `section` column containing this number.


(c) How many courses contain the word `Introduction`? Does case matter here?


(d) What is the longest course name (in terms of characters)? What is the shortest course name? Use `str_length()` to help you answer this question.

(e) Which course name is comprised of the most words? To do this, create a new column containing the words in each title using `mutate()` and `str_split()`. Then, create another column calculating the `length()` of the values in column you just created.


(f) Use `str_subset()` to return the course names that contain exclamation points (`!`).
:::

```{r}
countdown::countdown(10)
```

# Regular Expressions {.maize}

## Regular expressions

- Sometimes the patterns we wish to detect, extract, etc. too complex for exact matching 

    + Extract all time stamps of the form `HH:MM:SS`
    + Extract the string that comes after the dash (e.g. `hw01-aluby`)

- **Regular expressions** (regexps) are a very terse language that allow you to describe patterns in strings

- Confusing at first, but extremely useful

## Example

Suppose we wish to anonymize phone numbers in survey results 

```{r}
a1 <- "Home: 507-645-5489"
a2 <- "Cell: 219.917.9871"
a3 <- "My work phone is 507-202-2332"
a4 <- "I don't have a phone"
info <- c(a1, a2, a3, a4)
```

```{r echo=FALSE}
info
```

## Visualizing matches

The helper function `str_view()` finds regex matches

```{r}
str_view(a1, "5")
```

## `.` match any character

Find a "-" and any (.) character that follows

```{r eval=require('stringr')}
str_view(a1, "-.")
```

## `[]` match any occurence

Find any numbers between 0 and 9

```{r }
str_view(a1, "[0123456789]")
```

## `[]` match any occurence

Find any numbers between 2 and 7

```{r }
str_view(a1, "[2-7]")
```

## Your turn:

::: task
Detect  either "." or "-" in the `info` vector.
:::

```{r}
a1 <- "Home: 507-645-5489"
a2 <- "Cell: 219.917.9871"
a3 <- "My work phone is 507-202-2332"
a4 <- "I don't have a phone"
info <- c(a1, a2, a3, a4)
```


```{r echo=FALSE}
countdown(2)
```

## Special patterns 

There are a number of special patterns that match more than one character

::: nonincremental 
- `\\d` - digit
- `\\s` - white space
- `\\w` - word
- `\\t` - tab
- `\\n` - newline
:::

. . . 

```{r }
str_view(a1, "\\d")
```

::: aside
Outside of R, these would be single back slashes.
:::

## Caution!

```{r }
str_view(a1, "\\w")
```

## `[^]` match any occurence except

ANYTHING BUT numbers between 2 and 7

```{r }
str_view(a1, "[^2-7]")
```

::: aside
Common pitfall: forgetting the brackets
:::

## Anchors

Anchors look for matches at the start `^` or end `$`

```{r}
#| echo: false
str_view(info)
```

::: panel-tabset

### Entries that start with a digit

```{r}
str_view(info, "^\\d")
```


### Entries that end with a digit

```{r}
str_view(info, "\\d$")
```

:::

## Use regex in `str_detect`, `str_sub`, etc: 

```{r }
str_view(info)
```

```{r }
str_detect(info, "\\d")
```

```{r }
str_replace_all(info, "\\d", "X")
```

## Your turn

::: task

Fill in the code to determine how many baby names in 2015 ended with a vowel.

Use a regular expression to specify the pattern.
:::

```{r eval=FALSE}
babynames %>% 
  ___(___ == ___) %>%                       # extract year 2015
  ___(ends_with_vowel = ___(___, ___)) %>%  # create logical column
  count(ends_with_vowel)                    # create a frequency table
```



```{r echo=FALSE}
countdown(3)
```
